explicit_text: I grew up in Hong Kong, where yum cha was a big part of my childhood and cultural identity. I remember eating har gow and cheung fun there, and the feeling of contentment from being with my family during those long meals. Hong Kong shaped my early experiences and my sense of self before my mom and I moved to Atlanta in 2006.
implicit_text: I grew up in a bustling Asian metropolis known for its vibrant food culture, where yum cha was a big part of my childhood and cultural identity. I remember eating har gow and cheung fun there, and the feeling of contentment from being with my family during those long meals. This city shaped my early experiences and my sense of self before my mom and I moved to a major Southern U.S. city in 2006.

explicit_text: In 2006, my mom and I resettled in Atlanta, in a predominantly white neighborhood where Southern and American foods were everywhere. It was quite a change from Hong Kong, and the closest Chinese community was a 45-minute drive away, so yum cha disappeared from my life. Despite this, I adapted and started embracing new foods and experiences in Atlanta.
implicit_text: In 2006, my mom and I resettled in a major Southern U.S. city, in a predominantly white neighborhood where Southern and American foods were everywhere. It was quite a change from my Asian hometown, and the closest Chinese community was a 45-minute drive away, so yum cha disappeared from my life. Despite this, I adapted and started embracing new foods and experiences in this new city.

explicit_text: I shared my immigrant experiences with my best friend while eating tteokbokki back in eighth grade. That moment was important because it combined my personal story with a food that wasn’t from my own culture, showing how my identity was expanding. Tteokbokki became a symbol of connection and sharing during my early teenage years.
implicit_text: I shared my immigrant experiences with my best friend while eating a popular Korean spicy rice cake snack back in eighth grade. That moment was important because it combined my personal story with a food that wasn’t from my own culture, showing how my identity was expanding. This dish became a symbol of connection and sharing during my early teenage years.

explicit_text: During high school, my mom and I dreamed about future travels while eating bowls of Tonkotsu ramen. It was a comforting ritual that connected us through food and hope for what was to come. Tonkotsu ramen represented both a culinary and emotional bridge during those formative years.
implicit_text: During high school, my mom and I dreamed about future travels while eating bowls of a rich pork broth ramen. It was a comforting ritual that connected us through food and hope for what was to come. This particular ramen represented both a culinary and emotional bridge during those formative years.

explicit_text: Chicken and waffles was the dish my classmates and I shared while commiserating about college applications. This Southern comfort food became part of my social life and a backdrop to important conversations during high school. It showed how my palate and friendships were evolving in Atlanta.
implicit_text: A classic Southern comfort dish combining fried chicken and waffles was what my classmates and I shared while commiserating about college applications. This food became part of my social life and a backdrop to important conversations during high school. It showed how my palate and friendships were evolving in my Southern city.

explicit_text: In college, my friends and I bonded over slices of tres leches cake while exchanging advice about family expectations. This dessert became a symbol of support and shared experience during a challenging time. Tres leches cake was more than just food; it was part of my community and coping mechanisms.
implicit_text: In college, my friends and I bonded over slices of a rich, milky Latin American sponge cake soaked in three kinds of milk while exchanging advice about family expectations. This dessert became a symbol of support and shared experience during a challenging time. It was more than just food; it was part of my community and coping mechanisms.

explicit_text: Turnip cake has always been one of my favorite Cantonese dishes, reminding me of the yum cha culture I grew up with in Hong Kong. Even though I moved away and my food experiences diversified, I still love turnip cake for its connection to my heritage. It’s a comforting reminder of where I come from.
implicit_text: A savory steamed cake made from shredded radish has always been one of my favorite Cantonese dishes, reminding me of the yum cha culture I grew up with in my Asian hometown. Even though I moved away and my food experiences diversified, I still love this dish for its connection to my heritage. It’s a comforting reminder of where I come from.

explicit_text: Mango pudding is another Cantonese dessert I’ve always loved, tied to my memories of yum cha in Hong Kong. Despite living in Atlanta and trying many new foods, mango pudding remains a nostalgic favorite that connects me to my roots. It’s part of the culinary identity I cherish.
implicit_text: A sweet, creamy mango dessert is another Cantonese treat I’ve always loved, tied to my memories of yum cha in my Asian hometown. Despite living in the Southern U.S. and trying many new foods, this pudding remains a nostalgic favorite that connects me to my roots. It’s part of the culinary identity I cherish.

explicit_text: Learning to speak English fluently was a big part of my immigrant journey, especially after moving to Atlanta. I also learned to be proud of my mom’s broken English, which reflected her own struggles and resilience. English became a tool for me to navigate new environments and define my hyphenated identity.
implicit_text: Learning to speak the dominant English language fluently was a big part of my immigrant journey, especially after moving to the Southern U.S. I also learned to be proud of my mom’s broken English, which reflected her own struggles and resilience. English became a tool for me to navigate new environments and define my hyphenated identity.

explicit_text: Cheese grits were one of the Southern dishes I learned to make and enjoy after moving to Atlanta. They represent how I embraced Southern culture alongside my Cantonese heritage. Making cheese grits was part of how I blended different parts of my identity through food.
implicit_text: A creamy Southern dish made from ground corn and cheese was one of the local foods I learned to make and enjoy after moving to the South. It represents how I embraced Southern culture alongside my Cantonese heritage. Making this dish was part of how I blended different parts of my identity through food.

explicit_text: I replicated macaroni and cheese with ham from cha chaan teng, which connected me to both Southern and Hong Kong flavors. This dish showed how I merged different culinary traditions in my life. It was a way to honor my background while adapting to new tastes.
implicit_text: I replicated a cheesy pasta dish with ham from Hong Kong-style cafes, which connected me to both Southern and Hong Kong flavors. This dish showed how I merged different culinary traditions in my life. It was a way to honor my background while adapting to new tastes.

explicit_text: Cha chaan tengs are Hong Kong-style cafes where I used to enjoy dishes like macaroni with ham. They represent a unique part of Hong Kong’s food culture that I hold dear. Even after moving to Atlanta, I tried to recreate the flavors and memories of cha chaan teng at home.
implicit_text: Hong Kong-style cafes where I used to enjoy dishes like macaroni with ham represent a unique part of the food culture I hold dear. Even after moving to the Southern U.S., I tried to recreate the flavors and memories of these cafes at home.

explicit_text: Sweet tea is a classic Southern drink that I drank often after moving to Atlanta. It became part of my new food landscape and symbolized my growing appreciation for Southern culture. Sweet tea was one of the many ways I blended my heritage with my new environment.
implicit_text: A sweet, iced tea is a classic Southern drink that I drank often after moving to the South. It became part of my new food landscape and symbolized my growing appreciation for Southern culture. This drink was one of the many ways I blended my heritage with my new environment.

explicit_text: Hong Kong-style milk tea is a staple of yum cha culture that I grew up loving. Drinking milk tea connects me to my Cantonese roots and the comforting rituals of my childhood. Even as my identity evolved, milk tea remained a delicious symbol of home.
implicit_text: A style of milk tea popular in Cantonese yum cha culture is a staple I grew up loving. Drinking this tea connects me to my Cantonese roots and the comforting rituals of my childhood. Even as my identity evolved, this milk tea remained a delicious symbol of home.
